full_response:
Step 1.  Relate the proper time of the microbes to the laboratory time   
For an object moving at a constant speed v in the laboratory (un-primed) frame, the relation between the coordinate time interval Δt and the proper time interval Δτ is  

Δτ = Δt √(1 – v²/c²) , or γ ≡ Δt/Δτ = 1/√(1 – v²/c²).

The microbes must survive 3 h of proper time while 5 h elapse in the laboratory:

γ = Δt/Δτ = 5 h / 3 h = 5/3.

Step 2.  Solve for the required tangential speed v  
1/√(1 – v²/c²) = 5/3  
⇒ √(1 – v²/c²) = 3/5  
⇒ 1 – v²/c² = 9/25  
⇒ v²/c² = 16/25  
⇒ v = (4/5)c ≈ 0.80 c.

Step 3.  Convert the tangential speed to an angular speed  
For uniform circular motion at radius R (measured in the laboratory frame),

v = ωR  ⇒ ω = v/R = (4/5) c / R.

Thus the centrifuge must spin with

ω = (4c)/(5R).

( R may be given in metres; ω will then be in rad s⁻¹.)

Physical remark: ω is enormous.  For a 10 cm radius, ω ≈ 2.4 × 10⁹ rad s⁻¹ (≈ 4 × 10⁸ rev s⁻¹), far beyond practical capability—but this is the value required by special relativity.

\boxed{\displaystyle \omega = \frac{4c}{5R}}

Confidence: 90%
